diagrams.description: Medieval History studies the millennium between the end of the Western Roman Empire (c. 476 CE) and the early modern period (c. 1500 CE). This flowchart outlines the discipline’s logic: the goal is to understand how post‑Roman societies formed, transformed, and laid foundations for the modern world. It integrates core components: major civilisational zones (Latin Christendom, Byzantium, Islamic caliphates, East Asian empires, and peripheral regions); key thematic domains (feudal and manorial systems, religion and the Church, economy and trade, social hierarchies and daily life, intellectual and artistic achievements); and diverse sources and methods (chronicles and annals, archaeology, charters and legal records, art and architectural analysis). These are interpreted through theoretical lenses (debates on feudalism, Annales school, world‑systems, postcolonial perspectives) and organised around a historical arc: early fragmentation and new kingdoms → high medieval growth and institutionalisation → late medieval crisis, renewal, and the dawn of the Renaissance. The resulting insights illuminate modern state formation, legal traditions, cultural heritage, and intercivilisational exchange.
